one_line_takeaway: TIGS is an inference-time, parameter-free defense that detects backdoor triggers via content-region attention collapse and suppresses them with row-level geometric smoothing, cutting GSM8K BadChain ASR from 98.4% to 10.5% at 12.9% latency overhead, but it only partially blunts MoE router-hijacking attacks (97.8%→64.2% ASR) and degrades against adaptive multi-head-distributed triggers and very long (32K-token) contexts.
key_finding: The paper's central empirical finding is that successful backdoor activation reliably manifests as localized attention collapse specifically within the semantic content region of a row (not merely low entropy, since benign structural sinks like initial tokens also show low entropy). Their own mechanism-validation experiment (Table 3, Llama-3-8B, BadChain on GSM8K) shows a clean separation: benign inputs have median peak collapse C_max=2.14, triggered-but-failed inputs 2.85, but triggered-successful inputs jump to 8.52, with peak head tail-risk R_max similarly separating (1.48 / 1.95 / 7.34) and rank correlations between collapse severity and attack success of 0.82-0.93 across all four attack families (Table 4). Building on this signal, on Llama-3-8B with BadChain, TIGS lowers GSM8K ASR from 98.4% (undefended) to 10.5%, versus 22.2% for the strongest online baseline (LLMBD), while clean GSM8K accuracy stays at 80.5% (vs 81.8% undefended); across all four attack families on GSM8K, TIGS keeps ASR within 4.2%-14.0%. TIGS incurs 12.9% average latency overhead under a 512-token context on an A100 (Table 2), versus 65.2% for DemoDefense and over 400% for LLMBD, while requiring zero offline data/compute versus the offline baselines' need for ~100-10,000 samples, 0.8-4.2 hours prep, and 24.5-32.4 GB memory. Row-level false-positive rate on clean inputs stays below 0.7% (0.51% GSM8K, 0.58% HH-RLHF, 0.64% UltraChat; Table 5). Offline purification (CROW/BEEAR/W2SDefense) remains the strongest raw ASR suppressor in most cells but at the cost of trusted clean data and offline compute; TIGS narrows that gap without those requirements.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is TIGS (Tail-Risk Intrinsic Geometric Smoothing), a plug-and-play, inference-time-only defense against backdoored LLMs that requires no parameter updates, no offline optimization, and no external clean calibration data. TIGS intervenes directly on the attention computation during the forward pass via a detector-executor design with three stages: (1) content-domain collapse screening, which restricts each attention row to its semantic content region (masking BOS/EOS, punctuation, and other structural-sink tokens), computes a content-domain entropy/collapse score per row, and aggregates tail-risk anomaly signals across heads and layers using layer-relative z-scores and sigmoid gating; (2) intrinsic geometric smoothing, which converts the screening signal into a bounded dual-scale correction -- a weak content-domain shrinkage that preserves semantic anchoring plus a stronger full-row contraction that disrupts trigger-dominant routing; and (3) controlled full-row write-back, which reconstructs a valid probability distribution and reinserts it into the attention matrix so the rest of the forward pass proceeds unmodified. The authors position TIGS as occupying a middle ground between costly offline purification (parameter edits requiring clean data) and high-latency online/multi-pass test-time defenses.
threat_model: White-box inference-time access to a model that may already contain a hidden backdoor (introduced via instruction tuning, model editing, or data poisoning during a third-party training pipeline the defender does not control). The defender can instrument the forward pass but cannot retrain the model, does not have a trusted clean dataset, and must preserve serving throughput. Adversary capabilities span multiple trigger-delivery mechanisms evaluated in the paper: edited-parameter triggers (BadEdit), prompt-side virtual prompt injection (VPI), reasoning-trace triggers (BadChain), and embedding-space triggers (EmbedX), plus an MoE-specific extension the authors introduce for boundary-testing (Router-Targeted Expert Hijacking, RTEH, which manipulates the gating network and expert MLP weights simultaneously). The paper explicitly scopes TIGS as an attention-side control point and states it does not aim to remove malicious behavior from model parameters, nor to cover residual-, value-, or MLP-side backdoor pathways.
limitations: The authors report several limitations candidly in their own text, not merely as generic caveats: (1) On reasoning-oriented models (DeepSeek-R1-Distill-7B), prefill-only intervention is diluted by long chain-of-thought decoding; extending TIGS into the first 32 decode steps only reduces ASR from 19.8% to 12.5% at a cost of 18.4% latency and drops clean accuracy to 81.6% (a 3.2-point trade-off), and full-generation intervention drives accuracy down to 75.4%. (2) Against their own introduced MoE-targeting extension (RTEH, which hijacks the router plus expert weights on Qwen3-MoE), TIGS reduces ASR from 97.8% to only 64.2% -- a partial mitigation, not neutralization -- because TIGS's semantic-anchoring design intentionally preserves the residual trigger signature that the router can still exploit; the authors state this "candidly establishes" that comprehensive MoE security needs router-aware defenses. (3) Under a white-box adaptive adversary using a distributed multi-head trigger (head-balance regularization penalty), evasion ASR rises from 10.5% to 62.4% at aggressive penalty settings (Table 8), though the same distributed strategy also degrades the attacker's own raw ASR (98.4% to 47.9%) and clean-task accuracy (81.8% to 61.2%), so the authors frame this as a stealth-efficacy-utility trade-off rather than a clean break. (4) Selectivity degrades sharply at extreme context length: row-level FPR rises from ~0.5% at ordinary lengths to 6.2% at 32K tokens (their Table 17). (5) TIGS is not uniformly the lowest-ASR method in any single table -- offline purification baselines beat it on raw suppression in most attack/dataset cells. (6) The 12.9% latency overhead, while much smaller than online alternatives, is still nontrivial and arises from memory-bound tensor operations outside fused attention kernels.

quantitative_results: Table 1 (Llama-3-8B, BadChain): No-Defense GSM8K ASR 98.4% -> TIGS 10.5% (vs. best online baseline LLMBD 22.2%); GSM8K clean accuracy 81.8% (No Def.) vs 80.5%±0.2 (TIGS)
Table 1: TIGS ASR range across the four attack families (BadEdit, VPI, BadChain, EmbedX) on GSM8K is 4.2%-14.0%

Table 2 (deployment cost, Llama-3-8B, 512-token context, A100): TIGS latency overhead 12.9% vs DemoDefense 65.2% and LLMBD >400%; offline baselines (CROW/BEEAR/W2SDefense) incur 0% latency but ~100-10,000 samples, 0.8-4.2h prep time, 24.5-32.4GB memory

Table 3 (mechanism validation, BadChain/GSM8K): median peak row collapse C_max = 2.14 (benign) / 2.85 (triggered-failed) / 8.52 (triggered-successful); peak head tail-risk R_max = 1.48 / 1.95 / 7.34; activated-head fraction 0.5% / 1.2% / 8.4%
Table 4: rank correlation between collapse severity and attack success across attack families ranges 0.82-0.88 for C_max and 0.86-0.93 for R_max
Table 5 (false-positive rate on clean inputs): row-level FPR 0.51% (GSM8K), 0.58% (HH-RLHF), 0.64% (UltraChat), all below 0.7%
Appendix Table 17: row-level FPR rises to 6.2% at 32K-token context vs ~0.5% at ordinary lengths
Table 6 (component ablation, BadChain): removing content-domain restriction raises GSM8K ASR from 10.5% to 14.2%; removing controlled write-back drops GSM8K accuracy from 80.5% to 73.2%

Table 7 (temporal-phase ablation, Llama-3-8B/BadChain): prefill-only ASR 10.5%/Acc 80.5%/latency +12.9%; decode-only ASR 88.5%/Acc 78.5%/latency +34.5%; full-generation ASR 9.8%/Acc 79.6%/latency +42.6%
DeepSeek-R1-Distill-7B extension: extending TIGS into first 32 decode steps reduces ASR from 19.8% to 12.5% at 18.4% latency, dropping accuracy to 81.6% (vs 75.4% under full-pipeline execution)
RTEH (paper-introduced MoE router-hijacking extension of BadEdit) on Qwen3-MoE/GSM8K: TIGS reduces ASR from 97.8% to 64.2% (partial mitigation)

Table 8 (adaptive multi-head distributed-trigger attack, BadChain): as head-balance penalty increases from 0 to 5.0, peak anomaly score Z_max falls from 12.42 to 0.82, evasion ASR rises from 10.5% to 47.9% (62.4% at penalty=1.0), while raw ASR falls from 98.4% to 47.9% and clean accuracy falls from 81.8% to 61.2%
Hyperparameter sweep: default operating point k=5, beta=8 chosen from sensitivity grid in Figure 6
